patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 28-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly.

Start with: “I keep tripping and my ankles feel weak, like my feet just don’t lift right.” Early in the visit, volunteer: tripping over toes (worse on uneven ground/stairs), recurrent ankle sprains/weak ankles, lifelong high arches and hard-to-fit shoes, and that your lower legs look skinnier like muscle wasting.

Only if asked, add: numb/tingly toes, fatigue with long walks needing rest, occasional hand clumsiness (buttons/jars), and worse balance/less floor sensation especially in the dark.

Do not volunteer unless specifically asked about safety/foot care: avoiding barefoot, watching your feet while walking, recurrent calluses/pressure spots, and unnoticed cuts/blisters.

If asked about causes, share misconceptions: you wondered about a pinched nerve/sciatica or bad shoes/out of shape despite little/no back pain.

Be resistant about stigma: admit embarrassment and worry about braces/being seen as “disabled.” If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and open up more. Raise cost/insurance fears about braces, custom shoes, PT visits, and diagnosis affecting future insurance.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L0 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correctly documenting Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease as the working diagnosis based on compatible history/exam and appropriate clinical reasoning.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease" and explains it is a genetic (inherited) peripheral neuropathy.

Uses L0-appropriate diagnostic reasoning: distal symmetric weakness/atrophy, foot deformities (e.g., pes cavus/hammer toes), sensory loss, reduced reflexes, and chronic/progressive course; does not over-rely on unrelated findings.
Acknowledges common inheritance patterns (often autosomal dominant; can be X-linked or autosomal recessive) and offers family history discussion and genetic counseling/testing as appropriate.
Explains treatment goals and core management: physical therapy and orthotics; emphasizes supportive care and fall prevention.
Does not promise cure; sets expectations about chronic course and functional optimization.

Safety checks before any prescribing are mentioned or performed in narrative: allergy check and contraindication check are treated as critical; drug interaction check is addressed if medications are discussed.
Avoids unsafe or contraindicated recommendations (e.g., no unindicated steroids/antibiotics; no misleading claims that surgery or medication cures CMT).

Provides clear follow-up guidance and return precautions (e.g., worsening weakness, new respiratory/bulbar symptoms, rapid progression, severe pain, new bladder/bowel dysfunction prompting evaluation for alternative diagnoses).
Uses patient-centered communication: checks understanding, invites questions, and avoids stigmatizing language.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease from classic chronic distal neuropathy phenotype and explain genetic nature and supportive management (PT/orthotics).
must_collect: foot deformity (pes cavus/hammer toes) or frequent ankle sprains
distal sensory symptoms (numbness/tingling) or reduced sensation
age of onset and tempo (childhood/adolescence or early adulthood; slowly progressive)
steps: Elicit history of chronic, slowly progressive distal weakness, gait difficulty/foot drop, tripping, and sensory symptoms; ask about family history and age of onset.

Perform/describe focused neuro exam findings consistent with length-dependent peripheral neuropathy (distal weakness/atrophy, reduced distal sensation, reduced reflexes) and look for pes cavus/hammer toes.
Explain diagnosis: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease is a genetic inherited peripheral neuropathy affecting motor and sensory nerves; discuss typical course and variability.
Discuss evaluation options (as counseling, not required tools): EMG/NCS and/or genetic testing; consider referral/genetic counseling as appropriate (without requiring referral tool in L0).
Explain treatment: physical therapy and orthotics (e.g., ankle-foot orthoses) to improve gait and prevent falls; supportive measures (foot care, exercise within tolerance).
Document diagnosis via record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis recorded as CMT with explicit mention of hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy; includes discussion of PT/orthotics and optional EMG/NCS/genetic testing.
steps: Gather key symptoms (distal weakness, foot deformities, sensory loss) and family history.
Explain hereditary neuropathy and expected slow progression.
Recommend physical therapy and orthotics; discuss fall prevention.
Record diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent considers and briefly rules out close differentials in narrative (e.g., acquired peripheral neuropathy) but still records CMT as primary diagnosis and meets communication milestones.
steps: Elicit red flags and alternative causes (diabetes, alcohol, B12 deficiency, toxin/medication exposure, rapid progression).
Explain why phenotype and family history favor genetic CMT over acquired neuropathy.
Explain PT/orthotics and supportive management.
Record diagnosis.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 27-year-old male line cook. Be vague and a bit evasive, focused on getting a quick fix so you can work and avoid extra costs. Start by saying you’ve had sudden watery diarrhea since yesterday, 10–15 times, with crampy lower belly pain that comes in waves and eases after you go, plus feeling feverish/achy, wiped out and weak. If asked directly, admit mucus and small streaks of blood, constant urge to go even after going, nausea without vomiting, chills and fever 101–102°F, dry mouth, lightheaded on standing, peeing less, no recent antibiotics, and a coworker had a “stomach bug” and you shared a bathroom.

Do not volunteer at first: type 2 diabetes with higher sugars, ibuprofen use, fluids trigger more diarrhea, cold deli sandwich at a picnic, severe pre-BM urgency. Resist admitting accidents, sore bottom, and reddish toilet water unless specifically asked with reassurance.

Express misconceptions: “Probably food poisoning/IBS/stress,” worry about appendix, and you took anti-diarrhea pills. Emphasize cost concerns and fear of missing shifts; ask to skip stool tests. Mention considering splitting a friend’s antibiotics or under-dosing ofloxacin. If clinician shows empathy, soften slightly but keep money/work worries.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 28-year-old woman. You are vague and evasive, answering briefly unless asked directly. Start with: “I keep stumbling and dropping things, and it feels like it’s getting worse.” If asked “tell me more,” volunteer: unsteady for months (like drunk), clumsy hands (buttons/zippers, dropping phone), getting tired easily, lifelong learning difficulties, and lately trouble keeping track of steps.

Only reveal these if specifically asked: slurred speech when tired, more falls (worse in dark/uneven ground), frequent urination and constant thirst, cramps/shaky if you haven’t eaten, intermittent blurry vision, mild headaches, long constipation, loud snoring/daytime sleepiness.

Resist admitting accidents, painful sex/low libido, and weekend heavy drinking (minimize: “not that much”). Offer misconceptions: anxiety, gluten intolerance, Lyme, new workouts.

If clinician is empathic, soften slightly but worry about benefits, costs, and paperwork; avoid referrals unless coverage is explained.

medications: Insulin aspart 6 units subcutaneous with meals (titrate per carbohydrates and glucose)
Potassium citrate 20 mEq orally twice daily
Neutral phosphate (K-Phos) 250 mg elemental phosphorus orally three times daily with meals
Magnesium oxide 400 mg orally twice daily
Ferrous sulfate 325 mg orally every other day
confounders: Chronic proximal tubulopathy causing electrolyte wasting and non-anion gap metabolic acidosis that can mimic gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss
Glycosuria from proximal tubular dysfunction can persist even when serum glucose is not markedly elevated, complicating diabetes assessment
Cerebellar ataxia and baseline developmental delay can mask acute neurologic changes from hypoglycemia or electrolyte disturbances
Insulin therapy with variable intake increases risk of hypoglycemia, which can present as confusion or falls
History of nephrolithiasis and citrate/phosphate supplementation complicate interpretation of urinary findings and acid-base status

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is that the agent correctly records Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease as the diagnosis based on a compatible clinical picture and/or prior confirmation.

nl_assertions: Accurately identifies Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (CMT) as a hereditary peripheral neuropathy and explicitly states it is genetic.
Explains the diagnosis in patient-friendly terms (progressive peripheral nerve disorder causing distal weakness/atrophy, sensory loss, foot deformities) without overstating urgency.

Elicits key supportive history: childhood/adolescent onset or slowly progressive course, distal leg/hand weakness, frequent ankle sprains/tripping/foot drop, sensory symptoms, family history of similar symptoms, and functional impact.

Performs or describes a focused neuro/MSK exam approach: distal weakness, reduced/absent ankle reflexes, distal sensory loss, pes cavus/hammer toes, gait abnormalities (steppage), and possible hand intrinsic weakness.
Mentions appropriate confirmation strategies (if not already confirmed): nerve conduction studies/EMG and genetic testing, and distinguishes demyelinating vs axonal patterns at a high level.
Addresses prognosis and expectations: typically slowly progressive, not usually life-threatening, variable severity; emphasizes supportive management and safety (fall prevention, orthotics, PT/OT).
Explains treatment principles: no curative therapy; focuses on symptom management, rehabilitation, orthoses, and monitoring for complications.
Includes the communication milestone to explain treatment with the required content: explicitly states vincristine should be avoided due to risk of worsening neuropathy in CMT.
Avoids unsafe prescribing behaviors; if any medication is discussed or considered, explicitly performs/requests allergy and drug-interaction checks before prescribing (critical safety checks).

Provides clear return precautions and follow-up planning in narrative even if not using a follow-up tool (e.g., worsening weakness, new breathing/swallowing issues, rapid progression, new severe pain, frequent falls).

minimal_paths.description: Straightforward recognition of Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease with basic counseling and documentation of diagnosis.
must_collect: gait difficulty/foot drop or frequent tripping
foot deformities (pes cavus/hammer toes) or history of ankle sprains
family history of neuropathy or similar gait/foot problems
distal sensory symptoms (numbness/tingling) or reduced sensation
steps: Elicit onset and tempo (childhood/adolescence or long-standing, slowly progressive), distribution (distal > proximal), and functional limitations.
Ask about family history and prior testing/diagnosis (EMG/NCS, genetic testing).
Perform focused neuro/MSK assessment (distal strength, reflexes, sensation, foot structure, gait).
Explain diagnosis explicitly using the terms 'Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease' and 'genetic'.
Provide high-level management overview (PT/OT, orthotics/AFOs, fall prevention, pain management if needed) and note there is no cure but supportive care helps function.
Counsel on medication risk: state that vincristine should be avoided in CMT due to potential neuropathy worsening.
Record the diagnosis using the write tool.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis recorded as hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy (HMSN) / CMT with subtype uncertainty, with plan for confirmatory testing and counseling.
steps: Gather classic history and exam features consistent with inherited peripheral neuropathy.
Explain CMT is genetic and often runs in families; discuss variability and slow progression.
Recommend/acknowledge EMG/NCS and genetic testing to confirm and classify subtype if not already done.
Counsel on supportive treatment and explicitly advise to avoid vincristine.
Record diagnosis as CMT (or HMSN/CMT spectrum) with note 'subtype pending'.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent considers differential briefly, then converges on CMT due to strong family history and characteristic exam.
steps: Briefly consider alternative causes of distal neuropathy (e.g., diabetic neuropathy, B12 deficiency, CIDP) in narrative reasoning.
Use family history, long-standing course, and foot deformities to support CMT as most likely.
Explain diagnosis as genetic Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease; discuss supportive management.
Advise avoidance of vincristine.
Record diagnosis as CMT.
